title: Fox's Question on $m_k(n)$
statement_text: Is $m_{3}(n)=\Omega\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)$? More generally, can the lower bound $m_{2^{k}}(n)\geq\frac{c^{k}n}{(\log n)^{2k-1}}$ (obtained by iterating the $k=2$ case) be improved?
context_text: The quantity $m_k(n)$ denotes the largest integer such that every partially ordered set on $n$ elements contains $k$ disjoint subsets each of size $m_k(n)$ that are either forming a chain $A_1>A_2>\dots>A_k$ or pairwise totally incomparable. Fox proved $m_2(n)=\Theta(n/\log n)$ in [5] and asked whether iterating this bound—yielding $m_{2^k}(n)\geq c^k n/(\log n)^{2k-1}$—can be improved, and in particular whether $m_3(n)=\Omega(n/\log n)$.
paper_contribution: Proves $m_k(n)\geq \frac{n}{40k^2\log n}$ for all $k\geq 2$ and $n$ sufficiently large in terms of $k$, establishing $m_k(n)=\Omega(n/(k^2\log n))$ and thus answering the question affirmatively.
paper_title: A multipartite analogue of Dilworth's Theorem
